pattern: Because prerequisites form a DAG, the earliest a course can finish is its own duration plus the latest finish time among its prerequisites, so a topological sweep that propagates finish times forward gives the answer as the maximum over all courses.
hints: Model it as a DAG: build in-degrees and an adjacency list from `relations`, then process courses with a topological sweep (indegree-zero courses can start immediately).
Track each course's own finish time = the finish time of its latest-finishing prerequisite, plus its own duration; a course with no prerequisites simply finishes at its own duration.

As each course's indegree drops to zero, its finish time is already final -- propagate `max(successor's current finish, this course's finish + successor's duration)` forward, and the answer is the largest finish time across every course.

edge_inputs.description: a single course with no prerequisites
two independent courses, no prerequisites at all
a single course with many prerequisites converging into it
a diamond-shaped dependency where two parallel paths reconverge
two disjoint dependency chains where the longer one determines the answer
no relations at all among several courses

stress.description: random DAG (all edges point from a lower-numbered to a higher-numbered course, guaranteeing acyclicity) with random durations
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 50000))
    time_ = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    relations = []
    max_edges = min(50000, n * (n - 1) // 2) if n > 1 else 0
    edge_count = min(max_edges, n)
    seen = set()
    attempts = 0
    while len(relations) < edge_count and attempts < edge_count * 3 + 10:
        attempts += 1
        a = rng.randint(1, n)
        b = rng.randint(1, n)
        if a < b and (a, b) not in seen:
            seen.add((a, b))
            relations.append([a, b])
    return [n, relations, time_]
